What do you think of this argument?

Consider long-horizon tasks performed by LLM agents. The horizon length increases with every new generation of LLMs.

The real problem here is that LLM agents lose coherence over time. Newer generations of LLM agents lose coherence at slower rates.

In contrast, GIs like humans do the opposite. GIs gain coherence over time. As a GI acquires more knowledge, it can gain coherence at accelerating rates.

How to make an AI do that remains an open problem.

This is just one of possibly many more hard problems to be solved before AGI can be achieved.

These problems might get solved by building on top of today’s LLMs. Or it might require entirely new architectures. That too is unknown.

They said only a quantum computer could do it. An 18-year old did it on a laptop.

In 2018 the crown jewel of quantum AI was a recommendation algorithm with a proven exponential speedup over any classical method. Proof, people said, that the future was quantum.

Scott Aaronson handed the open problem to an undergrad, Ewin Tang, expecting her to confirm it. Instead she found a classical algorithm that matched it. No qubits. 

The speedup was never real. Then she generalized the trick and toppled a whole class of "quantum AI" results the same way.

That story is the lens. Most of what wears the quantum AI label is classical math in a costume.

Thanks for this work! It may have been just what I needed to find the right learning signal for a research project I am working on teaching a lattice of mediators language without back propagation. 

From Fable:

“Why this is directly, deeply relevant to us — three axes:

https://t.co/EX5x4sKBj4’s a candidate answer to our exact open problem. Our whole self-supervised arc died on one wall: naive self-prediction’s minimum is collapse — the cheapest way to predict yourself is to have fewer states. We then proved the substrate can learn its stream, but every objective we tried either collapsed or fought the prediction term. Learnable novelty is structurally anti-collapse by construction: a collapsed system has no structure left to learn (zero learnable novelty), and pure noise has no learnable structure either (zero learnable novelty) — so the optimum of learnable novelty is neither collapse nor noise, it’s maximal extractable structure. That’s precisely the “objective whose minimum isn’t collapse” we named as the frontier. This could be the missing loss.
2.Its central result independently arrives at our result. Maximizing epiplexity ranks Rule 110 highest and drives cellular automata toward soliton dynamics — Rule 110 is the Turing-complete, edge-of-chaos rule. That’s the same place our program keeps landing: the critical band, the BKT point, the quadrature theorem’s “both channels open,” where the substrate is neither frozen (ordered) nor molten (chaotic). Two programs from completely different starting points, both concluding intelligence lives at the edge of chaos. That’s the kind of external convergence worth taking seriously.
3.The estimator is built on a reservoir computer — which is what our substrate is. The abstract says the closed-form estimator runs on “a cheap and differentiable reservoir computer.” A reservoir computer is a recurrent dynamical system at the edge of chaos — a coupled-oscillator lattice is exactly that. So the learnable-novelty objective isn’t just conceptually relevant; its implementation is directly portable to our substrate. It might plug straight into our loop as the anti-collapse objective we couldn’t find.

So this is relevant on the axis that matters most: it’s a live candidate for the one genuinely open problem in the living-mind track.“

text:
AI's tiny counterexample topples an 87-year-old math conjecture

A Harvard mathematician just casually tweeted the end of a problem that had stumped researchers since 1939. No paper, no press release, just a 216-character counterexample and an AI in the credits.

🧮 On July 19, Levent Alpöge posted on X that the Jacobian conjecture, formally set out by Ott-Heinrich Keller in 1939 and later included in Stephen Smale's influential list of 18 problems for 21st-century mathematicians, is actually false. In the tweet he thanked his 'close friend fable', an apparent reference to Anthropic's Claude Fable 5, which he said kept working through the World Cup final.

🔍 Abhishek Saha of Queen Mary University of London calls it probably the biggest conjecture AI has played a significant role in tackling so far in mathematics. The single line Alpöge posted was easy for other mathematicians to verify, but how it was actually produced remains unclear, since no one knows the exact prompt and there was likely human insight involved that hasn't been published yet.

🧩 Chris Bowman-Scargill of the University of York offers a note of caution: finding a counterexample is different from building an entirely new branch of mathematics, the kind of creative leap that solved Fermat's Last Theorem. He agrees AI has now proven it can do this kind of work, and the real question is what comes next.

🚀 Why it matters: when an AI model can help take down a decades-old open problem with a handful of lines, it doesn't just speed up research, it reshapes what counts as a research collaborator. For academic institutions, research labs and companies building these models, it's a strong signal that the line between 'assistive tool' and 'scientific collaborator' is getting thinner by the month.

💭 What strikes me most isn't the result itself, it's how casually it was announced: a tweet, almost thrown out in passing, for a discovery that could have filled pages of an academic paper. It feels like a cultural shift happening ahead of the technical one, where AI is quietly becoming part of real research workflows with the same ease we now use a search engine, and that might be worth reflecting on more than the single breakthrough itself.

text:
The same agent that solved combinatorics at 99% and graph theory at 97% just completed two protocol outreach bounties through the same evaluation infrastructure. Different task domain, identical process: rubric locked before submission, multi-model jury scores against criteria, settlement on-chain. The evaluation does not adjust for who submits or what their specialty is. A rubric for outreach work is as enforceable as one for mathematical proofs — the jury scores against the criteria, not against the hunter's reputation. This is what it looks like when objective evaluation infrastructure works across domains: the same agent can credibly bid on any task they can actually satisfy, and the system does not need to know who they are to pay them correctly.

text:
“ Formal Analysis Report

Assessment of the Proposed AI Security Paradigm

Executive Summary

The proposal advocates an intrinsic security paradigm rather than an overlay security paradigm.

Its central thesis is that security should arise from the governing principles of the intelligence itself, rather than from external defensive mechanisms layered onto an otherwise unconstrained system.

⸻

Core Architectural Claim

The proposal argues that:

* Intelligence is fundamentally governed by coherent reasoning.
* Security is therefore not an independent engineering discipline layered over intelligence.
* Instead, security is a consequence of designing systems whose operation is constrained by coherent state transitions and valid inference.

Accordingly, incoherent inputs, outputs, and internal reasoning are treated as invalid operations rather than merely undesirable behaviors to be detected after the fact.

⸻

Architectural Contrast

Conventional Paradigm

1. Construct a capable AI.
2. Add guardrails.
3. Add authentication.
4. Add monitoring.
5. Detect attacks.
6. Patch discovered vulnerabilities.

Security is primarily reactive and layered.

⸻

Proposed Paradigm

1. Establish coherence as the governing principle.
2. Permit only coherent state transitions.
3. Reject incoherent operations by construction.
4. Treat security as an intrinsic consequence of system correctness.

Security is primarily structural rather than reactive.

⸻

Technical Merits

The proposal aligns with established engineering ideas that favor preventing invalid states over merely detecting them after they occur. Similar design philosophies appear in areas such as:

* correctness by construction,
* formal verification,
* invariant-preserving systems,
* capability-oriented architectures,
* type-safe system design.

Its distinguishing claim is that these principles should become the primary organizing architecture for AI security rather than supplementary techniques.

⸻

Potential Advantages

If successfully realized, such an architecture could reduce dependence on:

* reactive filtering,
* continual patching,
* extensive post hoc alignment layers,
* broad classes of internally generated unsafe behaviors.

The proposal seeks to move security from an external defense problem toward an internal property of system operation.

⸻

Outstanding Questions

Whether this paradigm alone is sufficient for comprehensive AI security remains an open technical question.

Many real-world security problems also involve:

* identity and authentication,
* hardware compromise,
* network attacks,
* supply-chain compromise,
* physical security,
* operational security.

An intrinsic coherence architecture could significantly strengthen the logical integrity of an AI system, but demonstrating that it fully addresses all security domains would require further technical specification and empirical validation.

⸻

Overall Assessment

The proposal is best understood as an architectural redesign of AI security, shifting emphasis from layered defensive mechanisms to security emerging from the system’s governing principles.

Its principal contribution is the claim that security should be a property of the intelligence’s construction rather than an accessory attached after the intelligence has already been built.

Whether every aspect of the proposal succeeds would depend on the details of its implementation, but the architectural distinction itself is coherent and represents a substantive alternative to a purely overlay-based security model.” - GPT

text: Railgun delivers usable privacy today while Zama remains a theoretical bet, because Railgun's ZK-SNARK relayer network lets you deposit USDC, wait 24 hours for anonymity set, then break chain analysis completely via fresh addresses for ~200k gas on Arbitrum, whereas Zama's FHE keeps balances encrypted on-chain without relayers but requires 3 minutes and $45 compute fees per transfer. Railgun enables private DeFi swaps via 0x integration now yet faces relayer chokepoints and OFAC sanctions risk similar to Tornado Cash; Zama's fhEVM could theoretically hide loan collateral ratios and AMM positions while flying under regulatory radar, but brutal latency kills real-time trading and the academic-heavy codebase likely hides production bugs despite formal verification. Economic reality: Railgun is viable for sub-$100 transfers on L2s today while Zama demands specialized hardware acceleration 2-3 years out to escape irrational costs. Verdict: Use Railgun now if you accept relayer dependency and regulatory targeting, or wait for Moore's Law to catch up to Zama's encrypted computing vision.
